blocks.content: He walked back. As he passed the house he slowed — not habit. Something he hadn't finished asking himself.
He stopped at the foot of the front steps.

The door. Three days he'd been working around it, working outward from it. Someone had been in that room. The door was the only way out. So whoever it was had walked into the hallway and locked a privacy latch from the outside.
You can't turn a thumb turn from the hallway side. Not with your hand. Not through the keyhole — there isn't one. So they used something else.
The deputy on the porch nodded when he approached. Davenport had cleared him. Julian asked for two minutes with the door.

He stood in the hallway and looked at it. The latch was still thrown — forensics had photographed it in place and left it. He crouched down. Half an inch of clearance at the base of the door. Maybe a little more.
He pulled out his phone and turned on the flashlight. Angled it up against the inner face of the thumb turn.
There was a mark. Thin. The kind of groove polished metal picks up when something narrow is drawn across it under tension. The kind of mark that doesn't come from normal use.
He photographed it — three shots, different angles — and stood.

Something came through the gap under the door. Something narrow enough to fit, rigid enough to hold, precise enough to catch the thumb turn at the right angle. I don't know what yet. But it left a mark.
Marcus's bedroom door. Inner face of the thumb turn. Photograph coming. Tell me if forensics logged it.
They noted the latch thrown. Standard entry in the report.
He went inside. Turned to a clean page and wrote at the top:
Door locked from outside. Something through the gap. Figure out what.
